Description: Represents a relative time difference and is often used to add or subtract calendar-based time such as days or months.

Description: Groups rows into buckets based on one or more keys for aggregation, transformation, or filtering.
Use Case: Group records before aggregating or transforming
Example Problem: Count the number of people injured, killed, and unspecified for every crash date.
Example Answer:
ex2_df = ex2_df.groupby('CRASH_DATE').agg(
  INJURY_COUNT=('PERSON_INJURY', lambda x: (x == 'Injured').sum()),
  DEATHS_COUNT=('PERSON_INJURY', lambda x: (x == 'Killed').sum()),
  UNSPECIFIED_COUNT=('PERSON_INJURY', lambda x: (x == 'Unspecified').sum())
).reset_index()

import pandas as pd

Returns: Extracts part of a text value by position.
Description: Pull pieces out of text, such as month, day, or year from a non-ISO date string.
Use Case: SQLite positions start at 1, not 0.
Example Problem: I have a date stored as MM/DD/YYYY text, and I need only the year.
Example Answer: SELECT substr(date_occ, 7, 4) AS year_text FROM crimes;
Import: CREATE TABLE crimes (date_occ TEXT);\nINSERT INTO crimes VALUES ('01/15/2024 12:00:00 AM');\nSELECT substr(date_occ, 7, 4) AS year_text FROM crimes;

Description: Build a new string from multiple extracted or literal pieces.
Use Case: If one part is NULL, the result can become NULL unless COALESCE() is used.
Example Problem: I have a date stored as MM/DD/YYYY text, and I need to rearrange it into YYYY-MM-DD text.
Example Answer: SELECT substr(date_occ, 7, 4) || '-' || substr(date_occ, 1, 2) || '-' || substr(date_occ, 4, 2) AS iso_date_text FROM crimes;
Import: CREATE TABLE crimes (date_occ TEXT);\nINSERT INTO crimes VALUES ('01/15/2024 12:00:00 AM');\nSELECT substr(date_occ, 7, 4) || '-' || substr(date_occ, 1, 2) || '-' || substr(date_occ, 4, 2) AS iso_date_text FROM crimes;

Returns: Converts a valid date-like value into YYYY-MM-DD text.
Description: Turn ISO-formatted text into a SQLite date result for filtering or grouping.
Use Case: SQLite returns dates as text, not as a separate date object type.
Example Problem: I have rearranged date text, and I need SQLite to treat it as a date-style value.
Example Answer: SELECT date(substr(date_occ, 7, 4) || '-' || substr(date_occ, 1, 2) || '-' || substr(date_occ, 4, 2)) AS clean_date FROM crimes;
Import: CREATE TABLE crimes (date_occ TEXT);\nINSERT INTO crimes VALUES ('01/15/2024 12:00:00 AM');\nSELECT date(substr(date_occ, 7, 4) || '-' || substr(date_occ, 1, 2) || '-' || substr(date_occ, 4, 2)) AS clean_date FROM crimes;

Returns: Extracts the month number from a date as text.
Description: Get the month from a cleaned SQLite date.
Use Case: The month is returned as two-digit text, such as 01 or 12.
Example Problem: I have a cleaned date, and I need the month number for grouping.
Example Answer: SELECT strftime('%m', clean_date) AS month_number FROM cleaned_crimes;
Import: CREATE TABLE cleaned_crimes (clean_date TEXT);\nINSERT INTO cleaned_crimes VALUES ('2024-01-15');\nSELECT strftime('%m', clean_date) AS month_number FROM cleaned_crimes;

Returns: Extracts the year from a date as text.
Description: Get the year from a cleaned SQLite date.
Use Case: The result is text, not an integer.
Example Problem: I have a cleaned date, and I need the year for filtering.
Example Answer: SELECT strftime('%Y', clean_date) AS year_text FROM cleaned_crimes;
Import: CREATE TABLE cleaned_crimes (clean_date TEXT);\nINSERT INTO cleaned_crimes VALUES ('2024-01-15');\nSELECT strftime('%Y', clean_date) AS year_text FROM cleaned_crimes;

Returns: Formats a date into year-month text.
Description: Group records by month across years.
Use Case: Useful because 2024-01 sorts correctly as text.
Example Problem: I have cleaned dates, and I need monthly crime counts.
Example Answer: SELECT strftime('%Y-%m', clean_date) AS month_key, COUNT() AS crime_count FROM cleaned_crimes GROUP BY month_key;
Import: CREATE TABLE cleaned_crimes (clean_date TEXT);\nINSERT INTO cleaned_crimes VALUES ('2024-01-15'), ('2024-01-20'), ('2024-02-01');\nSELECT strftime('%Y-%m', clean_date) AS month_key, COUNT() AS crime_count FROM cleaned_crimes GROUP BY month_key;

Returns: Creates conditional output based on rules.
Description: Convert coded or numeric values into readable labels.
Use Case: Always close the expression with END.
Example Problem: I have month numbers, and I want to display JAN for 01.
Example Answer: SELECT month_number, CASE WHEN month_number = '01' THEN 'JAN' ELSE 'OTHER' END AS month_name FROM months;
Import: CREATE TABLE months (month_number TEXT);\nINSERT INTO months VALUES ('01'), ('02');\nSELECT month_number, CASE WHEN month_number = '01' THEN 'JAN' ELSE 'OTHER' END AS month_name FROM months;

Import: CREATE TABLE crimes (category TEXT);\nINSERT INTO crimes VALUES ('THEFT'), ('THEFT'), ('ROBBERY');\nSELECT category, COUNT() AS crime_count FROM crimes GROUP BY category;

Import: CREATE TABLE sales (store TEXT, revenue INTEGER);\nINSERT INTO sales VALUES ('A', 100), ('A', 50), ('B', 80);\nSELECT store, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue FROM sales GROUP BY store;

Returns: Rounds a numeric value to a chosen number of decimal places.

Description: Clean unwanted characters from the end.
Use Case: Can remove specific characters.
Example Problem: I have codes with trailing X and need them removed.
Import: CREATE TABLE items (code TEXT);\nINSERT INTO items VALUES ('45XX');\nSELECT rtrim(code, 'X') FROM items;

Returns: Replaces occurrences of a substring within text.
Description: Standardize or clean text values.
Use Case: Replaces all matches, not just the first.
Example Problem: I have phone numbers with dashes and need them removed.
Import: CREATE TABLE contacts (phone TEXT);\nINSERT INTO contacts VALUES ('123-456-7890');\nSELECT replace(phone, '-', '') FROM contacts;

Returns: Returns the position of a substring in text.
Description: Find where a substring appears.
Use Case: Returns 0 if not found.
Example Problem: I need to find position of '@' in emails.
Import:
CREATE TABLE users (email TEXT);\nINSERT INTO users VALUES ('test@email.com
');\nSELECT instr(email, '@') FROM users;

Description: Create formatted strings like padded numbers.
Use Case: Works like C-style formatting.
Example Problem: I need to format numbers as 2-digit values.
Import: CREATE TABLE nums (num INTEGER);\nINSERT INTO nums VALUES (3), (12);\nSELECT printf('%02d', num) FROM nums;

Description: Handle missing data.
Use Case: Evaluates arguments left to right.
Example Problem: I have nullable names and need a fallback value.
Import: CREATE TABLE users (name TEXT);\nINSERT INTO users VALUES (NULL), ('Mia');\nSELECT coalesce(name, 'Unknown') FROM users;

Returns: Returns a fallback if value is NULL.
Description: Simpler version of coalesce with two values.
Use Case: Only handles one fallback value.
Example Problem: I have nullable scores and need default 0.
Import: CREATE TABLE results (score INTEGER);\nINSERT INTO results VALUES (NULL), (5);\nSELECT ifnull(score, 0) FROM results;

Description: Filter rows based on partial matches.
Use Case: % matches many characters, _ matches one.
Example Problem: I need users whose email contains gmail.
Example Answer: SELECT email FROM users WHERE email LIKE '%gmail%';

Description: Alternative to LIKE with case-sensitive matching.
Use Case: * matches many characters, ? matches one.
Example Problem: I need codes starting with A.
Example Answer: SELECT code FROM items WHERE code GLOB 'A*';
Import: CREATE TABLE items (code TEXT);\nINSERT INTO items VALUES ('A12'), ('B34');\nSELECT code FROM items WHERE code GLOB 'A*';

Description: Combines multiple SELECT results and keeps all rows including duplicates.
Use Case: Append datasets with identical structure where duplicates matter.
Example Problem: Combine online and store sales into one list including duplicate amounts.
Example Answer: SELECT amount FROM online_sales UNION ALL SELECT amount FROM store_sales;

Description: Combines multiple SELECT results and removes duplicate rows.
Use Case: Create a unique combined dataset.
Example Problem: Combine customer emails from two sources without duplicates.
Example Answer: SELECT email FROM customers UNION SELECT email FROM newsletter;
Input example:
CREATE TABLE customers(email TEXT);\nINSERT INTO customers VALUES ('a@test.com
'),('b@test.com
');\nCREATE TABLE newsletter(email TEXT);\nINSERT INTO newsletter VALUES ('b@test.com
'),('c@test.com
');

Description: Returns rows that exist in both SELECT results.
Use Case: Find overlapping records between datasets.
Example Problem: Find users who are both app users and paying customers.
Example Answer: SELECT user_id FROM app_users INTERSECT SELECT user_id FROM paying_users;

Description: Returns rows from the first SELECT not present in the second.
Use Case: Find differences between datasets.
Example Problem: Find users who signed up but never paid.
Example Answer: SELECT user_id FROM app_users EXCEPT SELECT user_id FROM paying_users;

Description: Creates a set of unique elements from an iterable.
Use Case: Remove duplicates or perform set operations.
Example Problem: I have a list with duplicates and need only unique values.

Description: Returns a new set containing all unique elements from both sets.
Use Case: Combine two datasets without duplicates.
Example Problem: I have two user ID sets and want all unique users.

Description: Returns a set of elements common to both sets.
Use Case: Find overlap between datasets.
Example Problem: I need users present in both groups.

Description: Returns elements in the first set but not in the second.
Use Case: Find items unique to one dataset.
Example Problem: I need users in group A but not in group B.

Description: Returns elements in either set but not both.
Use Case: Find non-overlapping elements between datasets.
Example Problem: I need users who are only in one of two groups.

Description: Adds a single element to a set in-place.
Use Case: Insert a new unique element.
Example Problem: I want to add a new user ID to a set.

Description: Removes a specific element from a set; errors if not found.
Use Case: Delete a known existing element.
Example Problem: I need to remove a user ID that must exist.

Description: Removes an element if present, does nothing if not.
Use Case: Safely remove elements without errors.
Example Problem: I want to remove a user ID if it exists without failing.

Description: Adds elements from another iterable into the set in-place.
Use Case: Merge another collection into a set.
Example Problem: I want to add multiple IDs from a list into my set.

Description: Checks if all elements of one set are contained in another.
Use Case: Validate if one dataset is fully contained in another.
Example Problem: I need to confirm all required IDs exist in the main set.

Description: Checks if a set contains all elements of another set.
Use Case: Validate if a dataset fully covers another.
Example Problem: I need to confirm my dataset includes all required IDs.

Description: Checks if two sets have no elements in common.
Use Case: Ensure datasets do not overlap.
Example Problem: I need to verify two groups share no common IDs.

Description: Returns all possible ordered arrangements of elements of length r.
Use Case: Generate all possible orderings where position matters.
Example Problem: I have a list of 3 items and need all possible 2-item orderings.

Description: Returns all possible unordered combinations of elements of length r.
Use Case: Generate unique groupings where order does not matter.
Example Problem: I have 3 items and need all unique 2-item groups.

Description: Returns Cartesian product of input iterables.
Use Case: Generate all pair combinations across multiple lists.
Example Problem: I have colors and sizes and need all possible pairs.

Description: Returns combinations allowing repeated elements.
Use Case: Generate combinations where items can repeat.
Example Problem: I need 2-item combinations where repetition is allowed.

Description: Returns number of permutations of n items taken r at a time.
Use Case: Count ordered arrangements without generating them.
Example Problem: I need the number of ways to arrange 3 items taken 2 at a time.

Description: Returns number of combinations of n items taken r at a time.
Use Case: Count combinations without generating them.
Example Problem: I need the number of ways to choose 2 items from 3.

Description: Chains multiple iterables into a single sequence.
Use Case: Flatten multiple lists into one sequence.
Example Problem: I have multiple lists and want to iterate over them as one.

Description: Zips iterables of different lengths, filling missing values.
Use Case: Align uneven datasets safely.
Example Problem: I have two lists of different lengths and need paired output.
